cluster_name: IMF cuts 2026 global growth forecast to 3% amid Middle East war
tldr: At the headline layer, the IMF's second 2026 growth downgrade to 3% generates broad coverage but fractures immediately into national scorecards: South Korea leads with its upward revision to 2.6% credited to chip demand, Argentina spotlights its confirmed 3.5% forecast, and Italy drops the global story to report only its unchanged domestic number. Six headlines explicitly name the Middle East war or 'geopolitical tensions' as the cause, with India alone using 'West Asia' rather than 'Middle East' — the geographic nomenclature divide visible at the title level without reading a word of body text. Japan's Nikkei Asia headlines depart from the monetary-institution framing entirely, covering an energy-shock-driven ADB cut and Taiwan's offshore-wind inflation headwinds instead.

evidence.quote: IMF lowers 2026 global growth forecast to 3%
IMF lowers 2026 world growth forecast to 3%
IMF lowers global growth forecast amid geopolitical tensions

frames.sub_frame: Middle East war as growth brake

evidence.quote: as Chip Demand Offsets Mideast Crisis
IMF lowers 2026 world growth forecast as West Asia risks
IMF confirms Italy GDP forecasts, +0.5% in 2026 and 2027
IMF confirms 3.5% growth forecast for Argentina in 2026
IMF Raises 2026 Growth Outlook for S. Korea to 2.6%
ADB lowers Asian growth forecast as energy shock hits
Taiwan offshore wind projects face inflation headwinds

outlet_isolation_top.note: Nikkei Asia's energy-shock and offshore-wind inflation headlines are fully decoupled from the IMF monetary-institution framing that anchors every other outlet in the cluster.
KBS World leads with South Korea's upward growth revision and chip-demand narrative — the only outlet positioning its country as bucking the global slowdown — diverging from peers' downgrade framing.
AFP/France 24's headline adds a 2027 rebound clause ('but projects rebound in 2027'), giving it a more balanced valence than the unqualified-cut framing elsewhere.

silences.what_they_covered_instead: Global Times ran a headline on China's 2026 defense budget (1.94 trillion yuan) with no reference to the IMF global growth downgrade.

single_outlet_findings.finding: China's sole headline in this cluster is about Beijing's 1.94 trillion yuan defense budget — entirely disconnected from the IMF growth story at the title level; its cluster membership reflects vocabulary proximity, not editorial alignment.

Both KBS World headlines foreground South Korea's upward revision to 2.6%; one explicitly frames chip demand as 'offsetting' the Mideast crisis — the only outlet advertising a country beating the global trend in the headline itself.
Uses 'West Asia risks' rather than 'Middle East' in the headline — a naming-convention divergence visible at the title layer alone, reflecting standard Indian government and press geographic usage.
Italy's headline reports only that its own GDP forecast was confirmed at +0.5%, dropping the global downgrade and war context entirely from the title.

Pakistan's two headlines split between a domestic milestone ($41.6B remittances, no war mention) and the ADB's Pakistan-specific growth cut 'amid ME crisis' — a headline-level duality absent from every other outlet.
The only outlet to use explicit war vocabulary in a non-English headline: 'guerra en Medio Oriente' — the most direct conflict framing visible in any title across the cluster.

bottom_line: The headline layer of IMF growth-downgrade coverage is nationally fragmented from the first word: countries with favorable revisions (South Korea, Argentina) lead with their own gains, those with neutral outcomes (Italy) drop the global story entirely, and war as explicit cause appears in six titles while Japan's outlets bypass the monetary-institution narrative altogether for an energy-sector framing. A reader scanning only headlines would see eleven different stories where a reader of the bodies sees one.
